brand_understanding.what_they_do: First-aid, health and child-safety education for parents and caregivers of little humans, founded by an Emergency Clinical Nurse Educator.
brand_understanding.who_for: New parents and caregivers — sleep-deprived, info-overwhelmed, googling at 2am, wanting practical confidence in moments that matter.
brand_understanding.voice: Warm, direct, practical, gently expert, anti-jargon, reassuring, slightly playful, never preachy.
brand_understanding.north_star: A member opens The Huddle with a specific worry, and finds the moment that calms it in under 10 seconds.
brand_understanding.huddle_promise: 5 experts. Real stories. Real talk. The next best thing to a parenting manual.

season.title: The Huddle — Season 1
season.tagline: Five conversations Megan had so you don't have to Google at 2am.

host.name: Megan Harvey
host.role: Founder · Emergency Clinical Nurse Educator · Mum of two
host.bio_short: Megan has spent over a decade in Melbourne hospitals teaching families face-to-face. The Huddle is the on-demand version of that — a non-judgemental space where parents can learn, refresh and feel a little less alone.

season.intro_promise: Welcome to The Huddle, the place where parents come together to learn, laugh, and feel a little less alone in this wild ride called parenting. I'm Megan from Help at Hand Education.
season.outro_promise: Hey, thanks for joining us. I hope you got something out of today and learnt a lot. If you love this chat, don't forget to tell your friends and family about The Huddle by Help at Hand Education. We'll see you next time.

guest.name: Claire Maule
guest.business: Bloom Physio (telehealth) · Epworth Freemasons maternity ward + outpatient pelvic health clinic
guest.bio_paragraph: Claire is a women's health physio of 17 years — she landed in the field on her last clinical placement and never left. Mum of three. Works on the maternity ward at Epworth Freemasons and runs Bloom Physio for telehealth consults. She's the person who walks into the maternity room and tells you bladder leakage isn't normal, vaginal heaviness isn't normal, and yes you can do something about it — proactively.
guest.rawness_note: Claire shares that she muddled through her first pregnancy despite knowing the science — felt tired, sick, juggling. She got better at it pregnancy by pregnancy. Honest about the 'I lived on toasties with my first' parent reality.

episodes.mission_line: Pelvic floor and women's-health physio — for the mum who didn't know bladder leakage and pelvic heaviness aren't 'just normal'.

questions.question_asked: What's your background and how did you get into women's physiotherapy?
questions.answer_excerpt: Physio of 17 years. Did her ACL in netball at 17, loved the rehab, went into physio thinking sports or paediatrics. Last clinical placement was at a women's health practice in East Melbourne — basically never left.

questions.question_asked: What did you notice once you became pregnant yourself?
questions.answer_excerpt: Did 10 years of women's health physio before having her own kids. Thought she was prepared, then was really sick with her first and 'muddled through.' Got better with each pregnancy. Realised the importance of eating well and exercising.

questions.question_asked: What are the exercise recommendations through pregnancy?
questions.answer_excerpt: Most days of the week, ideally all. 30 to 60 minutes — mix of cardio, strength, core and stretching. Walking, swimming, Pilates, yoga, stationary bike are all great. Lowers gestational diabetes and preeclampsia risk and helps recovery.

questions.question_asked: Why is the pelvic floor so important — even before babies?
questions.answer_excerpt: It's a sling of muscle — a hammock at the base of the pelvis — controlling continence and supporting pelvic organs. Pregnancy hormones plus the weight of the baby make the hammock sag. Quality matters more than quantity — feel the squeeze AND the let-go.

questions.question_asked: What's the main concern pregnant women come to see you about?
questions.answer_excerpt: A lot of pelvic-floor-related: bladder leakage with cough/sneeze/laugh, urgency, frequency. Bowel issues. Vaginal heaviness — often mistaken for prolapse. Pelvic girdle pain (often masking as low-back pain). Birth preparation including perineal massage.

questions.question_asked: What support wear should you actually buy — is more expensive better?
questions.answer_excerpt: Two categories. Medical-grade compression (the more expensive ones — bike shorts, leggings, support underwear with perineal gusset) and activewear-grade. If pregnancy is comfortable, activewear is fine. If there's pain, heaviness, vulval varicosities or muscle separation — medical-grade is worth the money, especially in the first 6 weeks postnatal.

questions.question_asked: When should people book in to see you during pregnancy?
questions.answer_excerpt: Three appointments ideally. One around 12–13 weeks for screening, exercise check, pelvic floor check. Mid-pregnancy when aches and pelvic pain often start. End-of-pregnancy for perineal massage, recovery advice, birth prep, support-wear planning.

questions.question_asked: What about abdominal separation — should everyone get checked?
questions.answer_excerpt: Don't worry about measurement during pregnancy — all tummy muscles stretch. Most hospitals have a physio that comes around. Otherwise check at 4–6 weeks postnatal. Some separation is normal; the question is whether you need specific recovery work.

questions.question_asked: Where can people find you and book in?
questions.answer_excerpt: Maternity ward at Epworth Freemasons. Outpatient pelvic health clinic there. Bloom Physio for telehealth — handy when you've got a new baby on your chest and want to check in from home.

questions.question_asked: As a parent yourself, what advice would you give new parents?
questions.answer_excerpt: Two things. If you've got a partner, work as a team — sit down at the start of the week and divide it. And fill your cup up first. Both Tom and I prioritise each other's exercise. If you fill your cup up, you're a better parent.

quotes.text: I didn't realize that bladder leakage wasn't normal, or I've actually got a little bit of pelvic heaviness.
quotes.attribution: Claire Maule · what new mums often realise too late

quotes.text: If you fill your cup up first, you're a better parent.
The hammock starts to sag a little bit as your pregnancy progresses. So if the muscle isn't strong, that's when bladder leakage can happen.
quotes.attribution: Claire Maule · on the pelvic floor

guest.name: Bianca Burge
guest.bio_paragraph: Bianca's a midwife of 13 years turned sleep consultant — Plan B was born out of her own two boys being shocking sleepers and the realisation that she could help families through what she'd been through. Mum to Oscar (8) and Hugo (6). Grounded in science. Brave on the mic — opens up about her postnatal depression journey honestly enough that anyone listening who's struggling will feel less alone.
guest.rawness_note: Bianca shares her postnatal depression openly — the 'overwhelming darkness in the evenings' after Oscar's birth, breastfeeding PTSD, getting it again with Hugo and recognising the signs faster the second time. She lost her own mum at 17, before her last year of school. The candour is the gift of this episode.

episodes.mission_line: Sleep, routines and self-settling — for the parent drowning in 2am Instagram noise about how their baby 'should' sleep.

questions.question_asked: Tell us about yourself and how you got into midwifery and sleep consulting.
questions.answer_excerpt: Two boys — Oscar 8, Hugo 6. Started midwifery 13 years ago. Did grad year in Deniliquin NSW, moved back to Ocean Grove for the boys. Started sleep consulting off the back of having both boys and them being shocking sleepers.

questions.question_asked: What were your challenges as a parent with sleep?
questions.answer_excerpt: Being a midwife, everyone expected sleep to come naturally. Put a lot of pressure on herself that everything had to be perfect. The pressures of being a midwife AND being a mum.

questions.question_asked: What tips would you have for blocking out the noise and judgment at the start?
questions.answer_excerpt: Don't put pressure on yourself. So much noise on Instagram and influencers setting expectations sky-high. The first 3 months babies are still in their fourth trimester — really enjoy that time and try to block out the noise. Everyone does it differently and it falls into place.

questions.question_asked: Are routines essential? When should you start?
questions.answer_excerpt: From around 4 months, routine starts to come into play because of the 4-month sleep regression — the progression to adult-like sleep. You'll start to see the wheels go creaky around 6–12 weeks when their melatonin kicks in.

questions.question_asked: What actually IS 'sleeping through the night'?
questions.answer_excerpt: Genuinely sleeping through — like 6:30 to 6:30 or 7am, no feed in between. Newborns can't be expected to. Around 8–9 months you can start asking 'is this habit, or do they need more solids/calories?' Adults wake every 2–4 hours too — we just self-settle without realising.

questions.question_asked: What's the most common question you get from parents?
questions.answer_excerpt: How do I get my baby to self-settle and resettle. It's complex — age, weight, routine, settling technique. Like a puzzle: if all the pieces aren't right, you'll be chasing your tail.

questions.question_asked: What age should parents reach out for sleep support?
questions.answer_excerpt: Bianca takes babies from 3 weeks for support — that's when colicky/refluxy stages can start and the newborn bubble wears off. For sleep training specifically, 4 months is when adult-like sleep cycles start and predictable routines work.

questions.question_asked: Can you share your postnatal depression journey?
questions.answer_excerpt: Always had some anxiety. Lost her mum at 17. Pressure on herself as a midwife — should be able to feed Oscar, couldn't. Sitting on the couch one evening: 'this isn't normal, this isn't baby blues, this is something else.' Got it again with Hugo, recognised the signs faster. Breastfeeding now gives her PTSD. The PND carved the way for Plan B.

questions.question_asked: What are the biggest sleep-training misconceptions?
questions.answer_excerpt: That you leave your baby crying with no response. Babies cry to communicate — it doesn't always mean something is wrong. Sleep training can be as gentle as you need. It's nothing like the taboo it's been painted as.

questions.question_asked: Where can people find your services?
questions.answer_excerpt: planb.com.au and Instagram @planbsleepconsulting. Weekly Q&As. Online packages, 1-week and 2-week packages with daily app support, in-home day or evening consults.

questions.question_asked: Final advice for new parents?
questions.answer_excerpt: Don't put so much pressure on yourself. Enjoy the cuddles and newborn snuggles. The grass is always greener — I wish I could go back to my first and tell myself it's going to be okay.

quotes.text: It's actually more detrimental to a baby's development for them not to sleep properly than what it is to short-term sleep train.
quotes.attribution: Bianca Burge · the line that settles a lot of families

quotes.text: I had this overwhelming kind of darkness that kind of came over me always in the evenings.
Whatever works for you at the end of the day. As long as they're healthy and you're loving them, it really doesn't matter.

guest.name: Isabelle 'Belle' Sayers
guest.expertise: Oral Health Therapist · 12 years · paediatric and adult
guest.business: brushing.with.belle (Instagram) · public + private practice across Melbourne
guest.bio_paragraph: Belle was meant to do commerce-law. A friend pointed her at the Bachelor of Oral Health at Melbourne Uni and she never looked back. 12 years in. Mum of two — Charlotte and Harry. Charlotte loved having teeth brushed from day one; Harry screamed and hated it. So Belle's been on both ends as a parent — and that's exactly why parents trust her when they say 'I think I'm hurting them, am I doing the right thing?'.
guest.rawness_note: Belle's honest about the screaming-toothbrush phase she lived through with Harry — gives parents permission to keep going even when their kid hates it. Now Harry pulls out lengths of floss before she even asks.

episodes.mission_line: Kids' teeth — first tooth to first dentist visit — and how to win the wriggly toothbrush battle.

questions.question_asked: How did you get into oral health therapy?
questions.answer_excerpt: Was meant to study commerce-law straight out of school. A friend mentioned the Bachelor of Oral Health at Melbourne Uni. Three years, then 7 years working, then back to do extended scope of practice for adults. Hasn't looked back.

questions.question_asked: What's the best time to first take your child to the dentist?
questions.answer_excerpt: Around the age of 1. First teeth come through at about 6 months. By age 1 you'll have all the incisors and possibly first molars. Plenty for us to look at — we also check soft tissues. Even without all the teeth we still want to see them.

questions.question_asked: Can kids really get cavities and decay?
questions.answer_excerpt: Yes. So many people don't think it can happen to baby teeth — and if it does, that they don't matter because they'll fall out. Decay is very common in kids' teeth. The stats are staggering. Important to understand the why so you can prevent or reduce it.

questions.question_asked: What about natural toothpaste — is it safe for kids?
questions.answer_excerpt: Natural toothpastes don't contain fluoride. Fluoride is a really, really proven protective force for preventing decay. Without it there really isn't much point using the toothpaste. Use a fluoridated, age-appropriate toothpaste — check the packaging.

questions.question_asked: What about flossing for kids?
questions.answer_excerpt: If their teeth touch, you should floss. A toothbrush can never get between touching teeth. Lots of flossing aids make it easier. If adults don't floss for themselves they often won't for their kids — but it's a matter of asking us how to use them.

questions.question_asked: Thumb sucking — when should you address it?
questions.answer_excerpt: Stop the behaviour by age 4. The first adult teeth come at 5–6 — if it's stopped by 4 there shouldn't be impact on the adult teeth. Tips: gentle reminders, distractions, the yucky-tasting nail polish (paint all fingers, not just the thumb!), socks at bedtime if they suck themselves to sleep.

questions.question_asked: What do you do if a child knocks out a tooth?
questions.answer_excerpt: Baby tooth knocked out completely — leave it out, see a dentist. Adult tooth — only touch the crown (not the root), rinse if dirty, pop it back in if you can. If not comfortable, keep it moist in saliva, water or milk and get to a dentist immediately. Time is critical for the long-term success.

questions.question_asked: What's the biggest myth in kids' dental health?
questions.answer_excerpt: That a hole in a baby tooth doesn't matter. Baby teeth stay in the mouth up to 12 years. Decay at 2 will not still be okay at 12 — it'll get to the nerve, kids end up in pain, infection. Baby teeth need fixing.

questions.question_asked: Strategies for parents whose kids resist brushing?
questions.answer_excerpt: Introduce something in their mouth as soon as teeth appear — wet cloth, toothbrush — so they get used to oral sensations. Position matters: cradle for babies, child-on-toilet hugged into you for older kids, child-between-your-legs on the floor for wrigglers. Same time every day, after breakfast and before sleep.

questions.question_asked: If you can only brush once a day, when should it be?
questions.answer_excerpt: Twice a day is hard. If you can only do one — do it at night. We eat, drink, breathe all day, our mouths are filthy by night. Asleep we don't produce as much saliva, and saliva protects teeth. Bacteria + less protection = trouble.

questions.question_asked: Final advice for parents in the thick of it?
questions.answer_excerpt: Parenting is hard. There are amazing parts and awful parts. Find the people you trust and listen to one — not all of them. There's too much noise. Once you find your people it makes it so much easier.

quotes.text: If you can only do one, do it at night.
Find the people that I trusted, and then I just listened to one person.
I know what to do dental-wise. But you know your child.
quotes.attribution: Belle Sayers · on parents trusting themselves

guest.name: Olivia McArthur
guest.expertise: Accredited Practising Dietitian · 12 years · paediatric specialist
guest.bio_paragraph: Olivia's a paediatric dietitian with 12 years' experience, specialising in fussy eating and the neurodiversity space — and she's a mum of FIVE. Got into nutrition after rowing through high school, when an early brush with disordered eating turned into a careers-counsellor moment that pointed her at dietetics. Started her private practice in 2018. She's the reason your toddler doesn't have to eat cereal at breakfast.
guest.rawness_note: Olivia is honest about the disordered eating in her family growing up and how it shaped her work. Also raw about the gagging-not-choking moment with her firstborn — out of the high chair, crying, baby smiling.

episodes.mission_line: Feeding little humans — solids, the fussy phase, choking vs gagging, and how to feed neurodiverse kids without losing your mind.

questions.question_asked: I struggle with mealtime with 2 kids. You have 5 — how does that work at your dinner table?
questions.answer_excerpt: The first child is by far the hardest because you're navigating it all new. By the fifth, I'm more relaxed. None of my five — and none of the kids I treat — has followed a book in terms of what they 'should' do at any exact point.

questions.question_asked: What's the biggest challenge with kids and food — what's the fussy phase?
questions.answer_excerpt: The 12–24 month mark is so hard. They're doing huge developmental growth, learning to be themselves, asserting independence — and you're also teaching them how to eat. Foods they used to eat get rejected. Textures change. Siblings influence. Back yourself: it's a phase.

questions.question_asked: What's your tip for introducing solids and making it less stressful?
questions.answer_excerpt: Around 6 months — definitely not before 4 (the gut isn't ready, allergy risk increases). Look for readiness signs: head control, the tongue-extrusion reflex receding, leaning forward looking for more. Milk is still the most important nutrition until 8 months — solids before then are for taste and texture exploration, not to fill them up.

questions.question_asked: How do you actually make introducing solids fun?
questions.answer_excerpt: Prepare for mess. There are 32 steps to eating — looking, smelling, touching, taste, then chew and swallow. The more you let them explore, the funner it is. Even if it doesn't go in their mouth they're getting sensory development.

questions.question_asked: Why does the high chair and posture matter so much for solids?
questions.answer_excerpt: Feet grounded, 90-90-90 position. If they're slouching or legs hanging they're using core to stay upright — that focus comes off the food. A high chair without foot support? Stack books or blocks. Comfort means they can focus on eating. Also a choking risk to be eating outside the high chair.

questions.question_asked: What's the difference between choking and gagging — and what do you do?
questions.answer_excerpt: Choking is silent — they could go purpley-blue, straight out of the chair. Gagging is loud, face goes red, often follows with a vomit. Gagging is normal — it's their defence when texture progresses. You actually want them to gag because that's how they learn to chew (tongue lateralisation).

questions.question_asked: How do you work with neurodivergent kids and food?
questions.answer_excerpt: Sensory profiles change everything. Super smellers, super tasters. ADHD meds impact appetite hugely. Often kids eat well for 12–15 months then drop foods as their sensory profile kicks in. Fruit, veg and meat are the most fibrous foods — chewing-heavy, sensory-heavy, all-different — and they're often the first to be rejected.

questions.question_asked: Should kids really have to eat 'breakfast food' at breakfast?
questions.answer_excerpt: There's no rule. If you struggle to get a dinner meal in because they're tired, offer the dinner meal at breakfast — that's when they're most alert and hungry. Try going straight from kindy/daycare to early dinner instead of afternoon tea. Maximise their intake when their appetite is best.

questions.question_asked: What are the biggest misconceptions about feeding kids?
questions.answer_excerpt: That you have to introduce vegetables first or every food separately. Iron is the key nutrient from 6 months (jumps from 0.7mg to 11mg). Allergens (cow's milk, eggs, fish, nuts, soy) should be introduced early and often — twice a week to maintain exposure. Most parents don't know that.

questions.question_asked: Final advice for new parents?
questions.answer_excerpt: Don't get advice from too many people. Don't compare yourself. Educate yourself enough to feel in control. With fussy phases, you're in charge — they're looking to you for routine and structure. Limit choices: four options for a 2-year-old is overwhelming.

quotes.text: It actually takes a typically developing child 15 to 20 times of trying a new food to actually accept it.
quotes.attribution: Olivia McArthur · the rule of thumb

quotes.text: Choking is silent. Gagging is loud — face goes red, often follows with a vomit.
quotes.attribution: Olivia McArthur · the line that saves panic

quotes.text: There are actually 32 steps to eating, from looking at a food to swallowing it.
quotes.attribution: Olivia McArthur · why solids take patience

guest.name: Felicity Conlan
guest.bio_paragraph: Felicity wanted to work with children and foreign languages — finished her teaching degree and a French diploma, then a friend's brain aneurysm pulled her into speech pathology. Watching that friend rebuild speech after losing it shaped everything. She taught grade 5 for a year, then did the Master of Speech Pathology at La Trobe. Now author of 'The Light Was Always There', a children's book about hope written during the pandemic with her students giving feedback page by page.
guest.rawness_note: Felicity opens up about the brain-aneurysm moment that redirected her career — watching her friend rebuild speech after losing it. Also notes how often parents come into her office and the session becomes a 'psychology session' about their own speech challenges as kids.

episodes.mission_line: Speech and communication — when to seek help, what speech pathologists actually do, and the at-home OWL strategy.

questions.question_asked: How did you get into speech pathology?
questions.answer_excerpt: Wanted to work with kids and languages. Started a Bachelor of Education and a Diploma in French. A friend had a brain aneurysm and lost her speech — watching her rebuild it pulled Felicity into speech pathology. Master of Speech Pathology at La Trobe.

questions.question_asked: How do parents know when to book a speech pathologist?
questions.answer_excerpt: Communication breakdown is the flag. Speech pathologists don't just look at stutters and lisps — we cover speech, language (expressive and receptive), fluency, voice, pragmatics (social communication), literacy, and swallowing. Anywhere there's breakdown, book in.

questions.question_asked: What's the most common concern parents come to you with?
questions.answer_excerpt: 'I think my child doesn't have enough words' — late-talker referrals. And 'I'm not sure if they should be saying this sound — what are the milestones?'

questions.question_asked: What age should parents look at speech sound milestones?
questions.answer_excerpt: Around 3 for sound milestones. F-for-TH ('thank' becomes 'fank') is the last sound to develop and shouldn't be intervened on until past 4. Stuttering has a strong genetic link — if it's in the family, see a speech path at age 2 or 3 because evidence is strongest with younger kids.

questions.question_asked: What area of speech pathology do you most prefer?
questions.answer_excerpt: Social communication. Working in schools, helping kids with breakdowns in peer and adult socialising. If you don't have the tools to be social, life is very difficult.

questions.question_asked: What are the biggest misconceptions in your field?
questions.answer_excerpt: TV stuttering looks one way — there are many ways to stutter. Speech-sound milestones: people Google American sources without realising the Australian accent is different. Use Speech Pathology Australia or ask a speech path directly.

questions.question_asked: What strategies can parents try at home?
questions.answer_excerpt: Interaction is key. The iPad doesn't talk back. Use OWL: Observe, Wait, Listen. If your child is playing with a train, talk about the train — follow their lead, don't make them say a word that isn't what they're engaged with.

questions.question_asked: How important is the parent's role in all this?
questions.answer_excerpt: The most important. Be present in the speech therapy session — the speech path is the model for you. Communication development happens at home and in everyday life, not in the 30 minutes with us.

questions.question_asked: Do parents come in and realise they had similar challenges?
questions.answer_excerpt: Often. Sessions become more of a psychology session about them as children. Speech pathology intervention wasn't common 'back in the day' — many things have a strong genetic link and opening up about your own challenges helps the support we can provide.

questions.question_asked: What's your final advice for parents starting to navigate all this?
questions.answer_excerpt: Communication is meant to be fun. Don't correct your child constantly — model. If they say 'I go down the street', you say 'yes, you went down the street'. Read books, sing songs. Have fun with it.

questions.question_asked: Tell us about your book 'The Light Was Always There'.
questions.answer_excerpt: Wrote it during COVID 2019, with her grade-5 students giving feedback on each page weekly. A picture story book about hope in the pandemic — wanted to give hope.

quotes.text: Communication breakdown — that's actually what we look at. Not just stutters and lisps.
quotes.attribution: Felicity Conlan · what speech paths really do

quotes.text: Observe, wait, and listen. The iPad doesn't talk back — interaction is the key to speech and language development.
Make sure it's not you correcting your child all the time. Model — they say 'I go down the street', you say 'yes, you went down the street'.
